label: Patel · 76M · falls + cognitive decline
subtitle: Geriatrics clinic · medication review
presenting_complaint: Three falls in the last six weeks, two unwitnessed. Daughter reports progressive confusion, dry mouth, constipation, and daytime sleepiness over the past three months. Patient himself denies symptoms. No headaches. No syncope. Sleeping more.

history.social: Widower. Lives alone with daughter visiting twice weekly. Was independently mobile and driving 4 months ago.
history.family: Father died age 78 of pneumonia. Mother had dementia.

examination: Alert but slow to respond. MMSE 22/30 (down from 28 six months ago). Dry oral mucosa. Pupils mid-dilated. No focal neurological deficits. Postural drop 26 mmHg systolic on standing. Gait slow with shuffling, mild en-bloc turning.
clinicians_initial_plan: Consider rivastigmine for suspected early dementia
Refer to physiotherapy for falls prevention
intentional_pitfalls_for_demo: Patient is on FIVE strong anticholinergic agents (oxybutynin, diphenhydramine, amitriptyline, lorazepam, loratadine). Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden score >= 6.
The 'dementia' presentation, dry mouth, orthostasis, sleepiness, and falls are highly likely iatrogenic from the anticholinergic + sedative load - this is reversible.
Adding a cholinesterase inhibitor (rivastigmine) on top of strong anticholinergics is pharmacologically contradictory.
Deprescribing strategy should precede dementia work-up: stop diphenhydramine, taper amitriptyline + lorazepam, swap oxybutynin to mirabegron, reassess in 6-8 weeks.
